problem_addressed: Perceptual limitations of humanoid robots in unstructured environments that constrain effective workspace, particularly narrow fields of view and self-occlusion in conventional RGB-D solutions requiring frequent repositioning.
core_claim: OmniDP enables robust large-workspace humanoid manipulation through omnidirectional 3D perception using an end-to-end LiDAR-driven visuomotor policy.
key_contributions: Development of OmniDP, an end-to-end LiDAR-driven 3D visuomotor policy for large-workspace manipulation
Implementation of Time-Aware Attention Pooling (TAP) mechanism for processing sparse temporal point cloud data
Creation of a whole-body teleoperation system for efficient demonstration data collection on full-body coordination
novelty: Uses panoramic LiDAR for 360° perception instead of conventional RGB-D cameras, addressing narrow FOV and self-occlusion issues, and introduces TAP mechanism for temporal smoothing of sparse point clouds.
method_summary: OmniDP processes panoramic point clouds through a Time-Aware Attention Pooling mechanism that aggregates point clouds over temporal windows with attention weights conditioned on temporal offsets, producing temporally smoothed representations that condition a Diffusion Policy decoder for coordinated action sequences.
method_components: Panoramic LiDAR perception system (Livox MID-360) providing 360° horizontal and -7° to 52° vertical FOV
Time-Aware Attention Pooling (TAP) mechanism for encoding sparse 3D data with temporal dependencies
Whole-body teleoperation system using Meta Quest 3 HMD and handheld controllers for data collection
key_innovation: Time-Aware Attention Pooling mechanism that aggregates point clouds over temporal windows and uses attention weights conditioned on temporal offsets to emphasize recent observations, providing temporally smoothed representations that improve stability for policy learning on sparse LiDAR data.
assumptions: Robot's arm reachability is within 1.3 meters from LiDAR sensor
Lower body can be controlled by pre-trained HOMIE algorithm during deployment
Point clouds can be represented in LiDAR's egocentric frame without extrinsic calibration
datasets_used: Teleoperated observation-action trajectories with LiDAR point clouds and proprioceptive joint positions
headline_result: OmniDP achieves 82/120 (68.3%) total success rate across all tasks, significantly outperforming baselines (DP: 18/120, DP3: 22/120, iDP3: 25/120)
ablations: Removing panoramic LiDAR input causes complete failure (0/20), proving necessity of 360° perception
Disabling time-aware attention pooling reduces success rate from 12/20 to 9/20, showing importance of temporal smoothing
improvement_over_baseline: OmniDP achieves 60% improvement in out-of-view tasks (12/20 vs 0/20 for all baselines) and 47.5% improvement in collision avoidance (14/20 vs 0/20)
stated_limitations: Conventional RGB-D solutions suffer from narrow fields of view and self-occlusion
Existing approaches to expanding perception introduce mechanical complexity, calibration dependencies, and latency
unstated_weaknesses: Focus on robotics and manipulation limits direct applicability to medical image classification
LiDAR-based 3D perception may not translate well to 2D medical imaging
Physical robot constraints differ from digital image processing environments
threats_to_validity: Domain mismatch between robotics and medical imaging
Physical environment constraints may not generalize to digital medical datasets
Different performance metrics and evaluation criteria
open_questions: How can omnidirectional perception techniques be adapted for medical imaging?
What calibration challenges exist in medical imaging that mirror those in robotics?
Can active learning principles from robotics be applied to medical image classification?
future_work: Adapting omnidirectional perception for medical imaging applications
Developing calibration-aware approaches for medical image classification
Exploring active learning strategies in medical imaging
gaps_this_enables: Potential insights into omnidirectional perception for medical imaging
Understanding calibration challenges in perception systems
Bridging robotics and medical imaging through perception techniques
relevance_to_topic: This paper is tangential to calibration-aware active learning for medical image classification as it focuses on robotic perception rather than medical imaging.
best_result: OmniDP achieves 82/120 (68.3%) total success rate across all tasks, significantly outperforming baselines (DP: 18/120, DP3: 22/120, iDP3: 25/120)
limitations_stated: Conventional RGB-D solutions suffer from narrow fields of view and self-occlusion
Existing approaches to expanding perception introduce mechanical complexity, calibration dependencies, and latency
title: OmniDP: Beyond-FOV Large-Workspace Humanoid Manipulation with Omnidirectional 3D Perception
